speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number six. March 24, 1996. My name is Susan Perez, speaking with Eli Stern.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're in-- we're in quarantine in Sweden, in a city called Helsingborg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Other transports came into Sweden from-- from Germany, and they were in various other quarantines. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's where they give us brand new clothes, because we still came in with the prisoner's clothes. And they separate us by-- by age, nationality, into different displaced person camps. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was put in the camp-- what we call the Romanian camp. Most of us are people that came from the same ghetto that I started out, from the same county and same state, so forth. We-- the sick-- the real sick, which were plenty of 'em-- wind up in hospitals. And everything on that, the bill is being picked up by the Swedish Red Cross. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After a while, being in this camp-- what we call the Romanian camp-- [INAUDIBLE] was the name of it-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took most of the young kids my age, and we were put in-- they called it a school, but it was more or less an orphanage home. It was-- we-- they had some classes during the day, but it wasn't a regular school. We were-- weren't-- we were given all the best. The food, the best of clothes. We're free to go about as we please. There are people in different parts of Sweden. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody is curious to see if we have anybody that survived and we wanted to meet. Somebody wanted to meet a brother, a sister, or a sweetheart. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So what we did is, they exchanged names of lists from different places in Sweden within different camps. So one camp, camp A, would see what camp B, the list, and so forth. And people did meet, because we came from different directions and-- after the-- after we were liberated. And there were people. Brothers met sisters. Sweethearts met their-- their-- boyfriend met their-- boyfriend met his girlfriend, and so forth. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] People that were in my place were-- were in the, so to speak, in a school, in an orphanage. What people there were older than I was at that time, they were allowed to go into-- into the cities. They could blend in with the-- with the population. They could get jobs, and they could start carrying on as normal life as possible. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I lived in Sweden two and a half years. And the end of '47, I left by boat. I came to the United States. I arrived the first week of January of 1948. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] After coming to the United States, I found out that my father is back home in our town, where we used to live before the war. At that point, the Soviet Union was-- took over Romania and the other countries in the area, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he couldn't get out at one point. He smuggled out somehow. He went through Italy to Israel. I was not an American citizen, so I couldn't take him out to the-- I couldn't take him-- send him papers to come here. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He went to Israel. From Israel to Canada. His younger brother that lived in Canada at the time-- that still lived in Canada at the time-- his name is [INAUDIBLE] Stern, who I mentioned before-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he helped him come to Canada. Now, he's in Canada, and I'm in New York. I'm not a citizen. He is not a Canadian citizen, and we cannot cross the border to meet. We found out that there is a way to meet at the border where we don't have to cross. I don't have to cross into Canada, he doesn't have to cross his. I imagine it's a so-called no man's land at the border. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] His brother, who lived in Canada at the time, brought him over to the border, and I took a bus. And I went to the border, and we met, out-- outdoors. It was seven years after we last saw each other. After that, we were in touch, pretty much, very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- What was the first thing you said to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a-- very emotional, crying. We didn't even talk. We just didn't know what to say to each other. It was a very emotional meeting, out in the-- out in the field. It was in an open field, actually. At-- at-- at the end of the meeting, he went back to Canada, and I went back to the United States. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- in 1953, I was drafted, and there was the Korean War. I was drafted into the US Army. I wasn't a citizen yet, but they made me a citizen after they drafted me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first came to America, before the Korean War, how were you able to deal with the language problem? Or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- there was an organization called HIAS. They-- they brought us over here. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was on a-- on a-- I came on a temporary visa at the time. They were lobbying in Washington. There were many, many people like us, at the time, that came over. And they're lobbying in Washington to change the law, to change us from permanent-- from temporary residents to permanent residents. And we-- were were lucky, and they changed our status.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you first think of Americans when you came over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: To begin with, I wanted to get away from Europe as far as I could, and I was able-- I was glad that I had an ocean in between Europe and the United States. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] America-- I wanted to come here. America's known throughout the world to be the land of the free, and I am very glad that I took that step to come to the United States. As I say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I came here, I didn't speak English whatsoever. I lived in an area where they spoke Yiddish. In those days, Yiddish was much more predominant in New York than today. They had several Jewish newspapers, several Yiddish theatres, and I was able to, pretty much, enjoy life, going to the Yiddish theatre, reading the Yiddish newspaper. Until I started to learn the language, I had no problem, really, because of the neighborhood that I lived in, in Brooklyn, a place called Williamsburg in Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- as I mentioned, I was drafted in 1953 till 1955. I was three years in the Army. While I was in the army, I became an American citizen, and I was able to meet with my father. I was-- I came home on a furlough. I changed to civilian clothes, and I went to Montreal. We met. And after I came out of the army, we met very, very often. Most-- a lot of-- most Jewish holidays, we got together. Pesach we got together. Most Jewish holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you exchange stories about what you both had gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I knew that he was liberated in Buchenwald. I told him that, after Buchenwald, when I left him, and we thought that we're gonna see each other a day or two later, well, I wound up-- and we just-- we never-- we never discussed-- we never talked about our war experience with each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We never even-- it never even occurred to us to talk about it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] although I still had nightmares, when I came to-- to New York, about the war. Just one example, I-- I lived in a furnished room in-- in Brooklyn. And during the night, on a-- on a hot summer day, we slept with open windows, because it was very hot. There was no air conditioning. And a fire-- fire engine must have gone someplace with the sirens on. I shared that furnished room with a roommate. And I was awoken in the middle of the night with that siren, and I thought we were being bombed. And I was going to jump by the window for cover. And he grabbed a hold of me. He says, you are not in Germany anymore. He was also a survivor. He says, we are in New York. This is a fire engine going to a fire, or whatever. And so, life started to become normal for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I came out from the army in 1955. I met her in 1956, and we got married in 1958. [? Twice. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife is also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I stop?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to ask a question.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- That's all right. You were-- you were talking about your wife and how you met. And your wife is also a survivor, as well as you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- We met shortly after I was discharged from the army in '56. We-- after-- after I met her, I saw she was alone, without any family. I asked her-- I found out, after our first or second date, what her background was, and we were both-- we found out that we are both survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk about it together as a-- when you were first married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Other than acknowledging that we're both survivors, we haven't talked about it [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for many, many years about our-- about our experiences. When our children were born, and got a little bit bigger, where they could understand, they kept on asking us, mommy, daddy, how come my friend has grandparents and I don't? How come my grand-- my friends have uncles and aunts and-- and I don't?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we told them a limited amount of what we could tell them at that time, that our families were lost in the war in Germany, and that-- we were-- but we never went into any details with our children, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] other than-- other than to tell them that we went through Auschwitz and all these other camps that we went through. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another fact. My wife and I, until this interview, where we sort of anticipated what we were gonna be talking about, I never heard my wife's details of her experiences, and neither did she hear of mine until just now. We just never talked about it. We-- we tried-- of course we will never forget it, but we tried to sort of shut it out, to live-- we lived in a-- somehow in a world of denial for a while, if that's the right word to explain it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has your faith changed? Or has it changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after we-- after the war-- I had a religious upbringing, as I mentioned earlier-- I just did not believe anymore. I just didn't observe. I didn't believe. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as-- as I got older, and as my children got older, I wanted my children to have a-- a Hebrew education as well as a English. They're both college graduates, my children, but I wanted them also to go to Hebrew school, and have a dual education, if that's the right word for it. And we slowly started to come back to become [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, you became orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when my children were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big enough to go to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] before high school. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What do you call it, the school before high school?

speaker: interviewer
text: Junior high school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Junior high, they started to go to yeshiva type of a school. And they went to yeshiva school ever since, until they graduated high school. And then they went on to college, regular college. But they were yeshiva high school graduates. They went to a place called Yavneh. They graduated Yavneh, which was in Jersey, and-- at that time, in [INAUDIBLE] New Jersey. And they graduated Frisch High School, which is located in Paramus, New Jersey. So they had the background that I wanted them to have. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they went on, from there, to college and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they got married, and they each have two child-- two children. My son has a-- a daughter called Jordana, and my-- and Jordana has a little brother, my grandson, who is named after my father. He's called Daniel. Jordana's named after her mother, [INAUDIBLE]. My father's name was Mordechai Hersch, and we called him Daniel. Sweet two children. My daughter has two children, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jennifer and Melissa. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We got, thank God, four beautiful, sweet grandchildren. My-- we had another blow two years ago, which I don't know which one was worse, the Holocaust or this blow that we had, where my son took sick with Hodgkin's. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he died in-- two years ago, November 28th, '93. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we're trying to cope with it as much as possible, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] watch our grandchildren grow up into beautiful young people, and we have a lot of naches from our children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know your grandchildren are going to watch this one day, Mr. Stern. What-- what would you like to tell them, as a survivor? So much-- what-- how-- how do you get through it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I-- I want them and everybody else, what I wanted to tell is, I'm telling my story in the remembrance of the family members that we lost, and in tribute to the six million Jews who perished at the hands of the Nazis. And I want them to remember, if there is anything as horrifying as the Holocaust, it is forgetting that it ever happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much, Mr. Stern, for telling your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. It's very nice of you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, you're looking at my father's parents, [INAUDIBLE] Stern and Esther Stern, my grandfather and grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken, or when was it taken, approximately? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea, but it was taken in Romania, in [INAUDIBLE], I would imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got it from my father. How he got it, I'm not sure. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is-- this is a picture of my father and mother when they were engaged. My father found this after the war and sent it to me, and I blew it up to this size. This is the only picture that I have of them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is another picture my father found after the war and gave it to me. And it's one of my younger brothers in front of our house. His name was Yitzhak.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately six, seven years old. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture [INAUDIBLE] my father found, as well, after the war, and gave it to me. It's my sister, Tobi, and my younger brother, Baruch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tobi is on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Tobi's on the right, and my little brother is on the left. It's somewheres next to my house, in-- in my town. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is a shot of my-- of me, the first picture after the camps, when I came to Sweden, early-- early May, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: In [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in quarantine at that point, in Helsingborg. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of me coming on the boat, coming to the United States. Somewhere's in December of 1947, at the end of December of 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: I noticed it says first class. Were you in the first class?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just took the picture to show that I was a first class on that boat, but I wasn't lucky enough to be in it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a shot of me when I was-- in 1954, I was stationed in Okinawa, Japan, US army. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is my wife and I at the night of our marriage, March 8th, 1958. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my dear son, Allen, July, 1972, bar mitzvah picture. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My son and I. I was given an award through the Rockland County Builders Association, and we're both-- he was there having naches on his father, and looking at me, and he was proud to take that picture with me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture of my daughter, Linda, and I in 1994, December, approximately a year after my son passed away.
